Last week, a source who happened to have been in Warner Bros. Discovery=E2=
=80=99s corporate offices on Park Avenue South relayed an intriguing observ=
ation: The executive suite where David Zaslav and his top deputies keep the=
ir New York offices had effectively been cordoned off, visitors were not be=
ing allowed onto the floor, and Zaz=E2=80=99s charges appeared to be in tri=
age mode. It may have been more innocuous than the source surmised=E2=80=94=
they may have simply been preparing for today=E2=80=99s devastating corpora=
te earnings call. But, in the dog days of summer, it was exactly the sort o=
f detail that provoked curiosity among both the Hollywood and East Hampton =
chattering classes and trouble starters. A game of telephone ensued as Zaz =
held it down in Paris.

Of course, WBD is a hotbed of intrigue these days, especially as the market=
 cap hovers
under $19 billion, with an enterprise value around $58 billion. The $7.20 s=
hare price now barely covers the cost of olives at the Hotel de Crillon. In=
 the latest indignity, WBD on Wednesday reported a $10.2 billion quarterly =
operating loss, which was driven by a $9.1 billion write-off for its TV net=
works business stemming from =E2=80=9Cuncertainty related to affiliate and =
sports rights renewals, including the NBA.=E2=80=9D The company missed its =
earnings by about $300 million. The stock dropped another 9 percent after h=
ours, at least as of this writing.

I=E2=80=99ve long argued that Zaz=E2=80=99s failure to keep TNT=E2=80=99s N=
BA rights would have severe long-term consequences for his TV business beyo=
nd whether Charles Barkley=E2=80=99s basketball expertise and charisma can =
be applied to the NCAA or the next iteration of King Charles. As today=E2=
=80=99s earnings intimated, the omission of the NBA from TNT=E2=80=94and fr=
om the overall WBD cable bundle, which includes many of those old
Discovery assets=E2=80=94has the potential to undermine his television busi=
ness entirely.

Zaz & Co. have spent a lot of time and energy in recent weeks going after t=
he NBA (which they=E2=80=99ve sued) and Amazon (which they=E2=80=99ve quiet=
ly smeared ( https://puck.news/a-pr-crisis-for-the-david-zaslav-era/?utm_ca=
mpaign=3DIn+The+Room+-+SUBSCRIBERS+%288%2F7%2F24%29&utm_content=3DIn+The+Ro=
om+-+SUBSCRIBERS+%287%2F19%2F24%29&utm_medium=3Demail_action&utm_source=3Dc=
ustomer.io&utm_term=3Df6c60617da42abd12a ), via Edelman). But as is now evi=
dent, his true nemesis has always been Brian Roberts, whose aggressive bid =
to bring =E2=80=9CRoundball Rock=E2=80=9D back to NBC now appears to have b=
een part of a brilliant and multifaceted corporate chess maneuver. By outbi=
dding Zaz at a price his debt-saddled company couldn=E2=80=99t countenance,=
 Roberts not only won coveted sports rights for NBC, but he also deprived h=
is competitor of the asset that allows TNT to command hefty subscriber fees=
 from Comcast and
other cable providers. (To be fair, of course, Zaz made a series of negotia=
ting mistakes that allowed Roberts inside the tent in the first place.) Tan=
talizingly, as my partner John Ourand recently reported ( https://puck.news=
/comcast-tnt-next-negotiation-will-be-awash-in-bad-blood/?utm_campaign=3DIn=
+The+Room+-+SUBSCRIBERS+%288%2F7%2F24%29&utm_content=3DIn+The+Room+-+SUBSCR=
IBERS+%287%2F19%2F24%29&utm_medium=3Demail_action&utm_source=3Dcustomer.io&=
utm_term=3Df6c60617da42abd12a ), we=E2=80=99ll get a chance to see the next=
 moves in this Vulcan chess match quite soon. Comcast=E2=80=99s agreements =
with Zaz=E2=80=99s Discovery cable assets expire next quarter.

Meanwhile, as one media executive put it, Comcast executives are now =E2=80=
=9Clicking their lips=E2=80=9D to =E2=80=9Cput the screws=E2=80=9D to WBD d=
uring the next round of price negotiations, when TNT and the Warner cable a=
ssets come up for renegotiation. =E2=80=9CThat=E2=80=99s going to be their =
entire play,=E2=80=9D another veteran media executive
said. =E2=80=9CThey will save money on the Comcast cable side of the house =
that will help pay for the NBA on the NBC side of the house.=E2=80=9D Or as=
 the analyst Michael Nathanson noted ( https://puck.news/will-venu-or-espns=
-planned-dtc-be-worth-the-money/?utm_campaign=3DIn+The+Room+-+SUBSCRIBERS+%=

tm_medium=3Demail_action&utm_source=3Dcustomer.io&utm_term=3Df6c60617da42ab=
d12a ) in a recent conversation with Ourand, assessing the competitive land=
scape for carriage fees: =E2=80=9CWhy is TNT $3 per month when USA is 50 ce=
nts or AMC is $1? I=E2=80=99m looking forward to seeing what happens in the=
 next year or two. I really am.=E2=80=9D

Comcast is also likely to have additional leverage over WBD due to CNN=E2=
=80=99s poor viewership=E2=80=94the network=E2=80=99s ratings in recent yea=
rs have reached an all-time low. And while C.E.O. Mark Thompson is saying a=
ll the right things about pivoting his business toward a digital
future, his bleak primetime ratings presents a short-term challenge. Shortl=
y after he closed the Warner-Discovery merger, more than two years ago, Zas=
lav described sports and news as the left and right pillars buttressing his=
 core entertainment business. After losing the NBA and depleting CNN=E2=80=
=99s audience, one of the aforementioned media execs argued, Zaz had effect=
ively eroded the leverage he would have had in cable negotiations.

In recent weeks, there=E2=80=99s been scuttlebutt that Zaz might be forced =
to split his company, spinning the television business off from the studio =
and streaming unit. On Tuesday, he and his C.F.O., Gunnar Wiedenfels, appea=
red to float a different idea, via the FT: a possible sale of some of the s=
maller assets in the portfolio, like Polish broadcaster TVN or a stake in t=
heir video games business=E2=80=94or, really, any non-core asset that a sui=
tor might be interested in for the right price. (Any split would be plagued=
 by operational
and legal challenges anyway.) But that alone wouldn=E2=80=99t be enough to =
stanch the decline in WBD=E2=80=99s fortunes, and ultimately Zaz may be for=
ced to offload a more meaningful asset.

Meanwhile, the loss of the NBA will also affect Zaz=E2=80=99s streaming bus=
iness. WBD already seemed like the tertiary player in the forthcoming Venu =
joint venture with Disney and Fox, given it was the only partner without fo=
otball rights. Without the NBA, too, it is now contributing a smattering of=
 far less popular sports that will decrease its share of the revenues consi=
derably. Indeed, WBD now seems like a third wheel. And, increasingly, it is=
: For a moment this week, WBD=E2=80=99s market cap came within half a billi=
on dollars of Fox Corp.=E2=80=99s, historically a much smaller company. Of =
course, everything at WBD is starting to feel smaller than it used to these=
 days.

Network News Switcheroos

Back in broadcast, CBS News has implemented yet another convoluted, multihe=
aded, McKinsey-style leadership structure, providing further confirmation t=
hat the industry has all but abandoned its creative ambitions and is simply=
 managing its own decline. CBS News C.E.O. Wendy McMahon will now oversee t=
he network=E2=80=99s national news broadcasts directly, while her lieutenan=
t Adrienne Roark has been elevated to president of editorial and newsgather=
ing for both CBS News and its stations, as I first presaged two weeks ago. =
Meanwhile, Jennifer Mitchell will become president of stations, as well as =
president of digital for CBS News and Stations.

Confused yet? Imagine trying to explain it to Roone Arledge=E2=80=A6 or to =
Andy Rooney, for that matter. At this point, CBS=E2=80=99s parentco, its ne=
ws division, and its
flagship evening news broadcast are all led by neutered three-headed manage=
ment structures, which says something about the state of the business, sure=
, but also about the shortage of available talent.

Meanwhile, over at ABC News, Deb OConnell is getting ready to announce her =
own post-Kim Godwin leadership structure, though she may wait to do so unti=
l after the network=E2=80=99s planned presidential debate in September (if =
that event still takes place). The strong expectation among my sources is t=
hat the job will go to Tom Cibrowski, the president of the San Francisco af=
filiate, though some are holding out hope for Good Morning America executiv=
e producer Simone Swink. And some fear a multiperson, CBS-style arrangement=
, which almost all of them said they would oppose. =E2=80=9CMassive mistake=
,=E2=80=9D as one ABC News veteran put it.

A Note on Axios

Meanwhile, Axios has proved to be one of the few true success stories in th=
e news business in recent years. As everyone knows, co-founders Jim VandeHe=
i, Mike Allen, and Roy Schwartz redeployed some of their keenest insights f=
rom Politico, albeit with a twist, to create their email-first, smart brevi=
ty empire on the banks of the Potomac. And while most of the conversation a=
bout Axios=E2=80=94including some eye-rolling and player-hating among schad=
enfreude-minded peers=E2=80=94came down to the obvious bullet-point-laden f=
ormat innovation, the company=E2=80=99s most profound impact may have been =
its reshaping of the D.C. advertising market.

VandeHei had basically crafted at Politico and perfected at Axios a new kin=
d of advertising business model: Axios content was B2C on the outside but v=
ery much B2B on the inside, and the email
delivery made it appealing to Fortune 500 companies and trade groups lookin=
g to influence legislators or push their brand story in the capital=E2=80=
=94a growing imperative in the Trump years. In many ways, these were the be=
st possible clients=E2=80=94engaged, active, recurring=E2=80=94and their cr=
eative campaigns looked better in Axios than anywhere else.

Creating content for the mobile era, and monetizing it with optimized adver=
tising=E2=80=94that was the insight that helped power Axios=E2=80=99s sale =
to Cox, announced almost exactly two years ago, for $525 million, or around=
 5x revenue=E2=80=94a healthy ratio for an ad-supported business. And some =
lingering jealousy over that sale probably also explains why many ears perk=
ed up in the industry when the Times reported earlier this week that the co=
mpany would be laying off 50 employees, or around 10 percent of its staff.

On some level, this news may be a response to some jejune factors=E2=80=94a=
 slow Q2 in the corporate affairs
advertising sector, the rise of new incumbents who have implemented similar=
 playbooks, and the reality that, under Cox=E2=80=99s ownership, some roles=
 just don=E2=80=99t need to exist anymore. In his note to his staff, VandeH=
ei went a step further, too, acknowledging that the profound sweep of A.I. =
was simply going to obfuscate some functions: This was a good time to cut b=
ait on bets that weren=E2=80=99t working and double down on what was. (Reme=
mber, headcount is certainly not the measure by which C.E.O.s value their c=
ompanies=E2=80=A6)

VandeHei is an honest broker and always has been. In fact, one of the reaso=
ns he=E2=80=99s occasionally been polarizing in the industry is the fact th=
at he shoots from the hip, and often with heavy conviction. Sometimes it ca=
n sound a little woo woo, as I=E2=80=99ve reported in the past ( https://pu=
ck.news/gospel-of-jim-vandehei/?utm_campaign=3DIn+The+Room+-+SUBSCRIBERS+%2=
88%2F7%2F24%29&utm_content=3DIn+The+Room+-+SUBSCRIBERS+%287%2F19%2F24%29&ut=
m_medium=3Demail_action&utm_source=3Dcustomer.io&utm_term=3Df6c60617da42abd=
12a ). But other times, like now, it can seem refreshing or even overdue. A=
fter all, we=E2=80=99re living through a moment in the industry when so man=
y executives and leaders, often wrought from a previous age, seem vexed by =
the present and forthcoming challenges. That has led to a series of harebra=
ined strategies and tactics=E2=80=94paying people more to do much less, pay=
ing them not much more to do far more; the aforementioned multiheaded manag=
ement structures that ensure nothing gets
accomplished; splitting newsrooms; signing sucker deals with A.I. models; w=
himsically shifting paywall strategies=E2=80=A6 I could go on. In his note =
to his staff, VandeHei acknowledged that =E2=80=9Cthis is the most difficul=
t moment for media in our lifetime.=E2=80=9D At least someone is willing to=
 say the quiet part out loud.

ghtly longer than usual to respond. Otherwise, the best way to get in touch=

p;P 500 is up 80 percent in the past five years, <i>including</i> Monday=E2=
=80=99s sell-off. Is that something to be freaking out about, or should we =
be thanking our good fortune for having been invested in the U.S. stock mar=
ket during that time? Perhaps certain CNBC guests should take a chill pill.=

gy companies that have been hyping their

billions, and in some cases trillions, of dollars onto their enterprise val=
ues. That was unsustainable, obviously. <br/><br/>I also think the reports =

 suite where <b>David Zaslav </b>and his top deputies keep their New York o=

all. But, in the dog days of summer, it was exactly the sort of detail that=

asses <i>and</i> trouble starters. A game of telephone ensued as Zaz held i=
t down in Paris.<br/><br/>Of course, WBD is a hotbed of intrigue these days=

works business stemming from =E2=80=9Cuncertainty related to affiliate and =
sports rights renewals, including the NBA.=E2=80=9D The company missed its =
earnings by about $300 million. The stock dropped another 9 percent after h=
ours, at least as of this writing.<br/><br/>I=E2=80=99ve long argued that Z=
az=E2=80=99s failure to keep TNT=E2=80=99s NBA rights would have <i>severe<=

ll WBD cable bundle, which includes many of those old
Discovery assets=E2=80=94has the potential to undermine his television busi=
ness <i>entirely</i>.<br/><br/>Zaz & Co. have spent a lot of time and e=
nergy in recent weeks going after the NBA (which they=E2=80=99ve sued) and =

          <td style=3D"padding-bottom: 36px; color: #000000; font-size: 16p=
x; line-height: 30px; margin-top: 0; margin-bottom: 0;">Back in broadcast, =
CBS News has implemented yet another convoluted, multiheaded, McKinsey-styl=
e leadership structure, providing further confirmation that the industry ha=
s all but abandoned its creative ambitions and is simply managing its own d=
ecline. CBS News C.E.O. <b>Wendy McMahon </b>will now oversee the network=
=E2=80=99s national news broadcasts directly, while her lieutenant <b>Adrie=
nne Roark </b>has been elevated to president of editorial and newsgathering=
 for both CBS News and its stations, as I first presaged two weeks ago. Mea=
nwhile, <b>Jennifer Mitchell </b>will become president of stations, as well=
 as president of digital for CBS News and Stations. <br/><br/><i>Confused y=
et? </i>Imagine trying to explain it to <b>Roone Arledge</b>=E2=80=A6 or to=
 <b>Andy Rooney</b>, for that matter. At this point, CBS=E2=80=99s parentco=
, its news division, <i>and </i>its
flagship evening news broadcast are all led by neutered three-headed manage=
ment structures, which says something about the state of the business, sure=
, but also about the shortage of available talent.<br/><br/>Meanwhile, over=
 at ABC News, <b>Deb OConnell</b> is getting ready to announce her own post=
-<b>Kim Godwin</b> leadership structure, though she may wait to do so until=
 <i>after </i>the network=E2=80=99s planned presidential debate in Septembe=
r (if that event still takes place). The strong expectation among my source=
s is that the job will go to <b>Tom Cibrowski</b>, the president of the San=
 Francisco affiliate, though some are holding out hope for <i>Good Morning =
America </i>executive producer <b>Simone Swink</b>. And some fear a multipe=
rson, CBS-style arrangement, which almost all of them said they would oppos=
e. =E2=80=9CMassive mistake,=E2=80=9D as one ABC News veteran put it.<br/><=

          <td style=3D"padding-bottom: 36px; color: #000000; font-size: 16p=
x; line-height: 30px; margin-top: 0; margin-bottom: 0;">Meanwhile, Axios ha=
s proved to be one of the few true success stories in the news business in =
recent years. As everyone knows, co-founders <b>Jim VandeHei</b>, <b>Mike A=
llen</b>, and <b>Roy Schwartz </b>redeployed some of their keenest insights=
 from Politico, albeit with a twist, to create their email-first, smart bre=
vity empire on the banks of the Potomac. And while most of the conversation=
 about Axios=E2=80=94including some eye-rolling and player-hating among <i>=
schadenfreude</i>-minded peers=E2=80=94came down to the obvious bullet-poin=
t-laden format innovation, the company=E2=80=99s most profound impact may h=
ave been its reshaping of the D.C. advertising market. <br/><br/>VandeHei h=
ad basically crafted at Politico and perfected at Axios a new kind of adver=
tising business model: Axios content was B2C on the outside but very much B=
2B on the inside, and the email
delivery made it appealing to Fortune 500 companies and trade groups lookin=
g to influence legislators or push their brand story in the capital=E2=80=
=94a growing imperative in the <b>Trump </b>years. In many ways, these were=
 the best possible clients=E2=80=94engaged, active, recurring=E2=80=94and t=
heir creative campaigns looked better in Axios than anywhere else.<br/><br/=
>Creating content for the mobile era, and monetizing it with optimized adve=
rtising=E2=80=94<i>that </i>was the insight that helped power Axios=E2=80=
=99s sale to Cox, announced almost exactly two years ago, for $525 million,=
 or around 5x revenue=E2=80=94a healthy ratio for an ad-supported business.=
 And some lingering jealousy over that sale probably also explains why many=
 ears perked up in the industry when the <i>Times </i>reported earlier this=
 week that the company would be laying off 50 employees, or around 10 perce=
nt of its staff.<br/><br/>On some level, this news may be a response to som=
e jejune factors=E2=80=94a slow Q2 in the corporate affairs
advertising sector, the rise of new incumbents who have implemented similar=
 playbooks, and the reality that, under Cox=E2=80=99s ownership, some roles=
 just don=E2=80=99t need to exist anymore. In his note to his staff, VandeH=
ei went a step further, too, acknowledging that the profound sweep of A.I. =
was simply going to obfuscate some functions: This was a good time to cut b=
ait on bets that weren=E2=80=99t working and double down on what was. (Reme=
mber, headcount is certainly not the measure by which C.E.O.s value their c=
ompanies=E2=80=A6)<br/><br/>VandeHei is an honest broker and always has bee=
n. In fact, one of the reasons he=E2=80=99s occasionally been polarizing in=
 the industry is the fact that he shoots from the hip, and often with heavy=
 conviction. Sometimes it can sound a little woo woo, as I=E2=80=99ve <u><a=
 href=3D"https://email.puck.news/e/c/eyJlbWFpbF9pZCI6ImRnVDJ4Z1lEQUt2Uktxcl=
JLZ0dSTDNNczdFbndab2tZaHZkRVNoST0iLCJocmVmIjoiaHR0cHM6Ly9wdWNrLm5ld3MvZ29zc=
GVsLW9mLWppbS12YW5kZWhlaS8_dXRtX2NhbXBhaWduPUluK1RoZStSb29tKy0rU1VCU0NSSUJF=
UlMrJTI4OCUyRjclMkYyNCUyOVx1MDAyNnV0bV9jb250ZW50PUluK1RoZStSb29tKy0rU1VCU0N=
SSUJFUlMrJTI4NyUyRjE5JTJGMjQlMjlcdTAwMjZ1dG1fbWVkaXVtPWVtYWlsX2FjdGlvblx1MD=
AyNnV0bV9zb3VyY2U9Y3VzdG9tZXIuaW9cdTAwMjZ1dG1fdGVybT1mNmM2MDYxN2RhNDJhYmQxM=
mEiLCJpbnRlcm5hbCI6ImY2YzYwNjE3ZGE0MmFiZDEyYSIsImxpbmtfaWQiOjg1NzYwNn0/af3d=
0c5db6ae94028a2a86ad9342754981d6f1ad70e78f454b865581a5b04736" style=3D"colo=
r:#d40000;text-decoration:none;font-weight:bold" class=3D"sdf">reported in =
the past</a></u>. But other times, like now, it can seem refreshing or even=
 overdue. After all, we=E2=80=99re living through a moment in the industry =
when so many executives and leaders, often wrought from a previous age, see=
m vexed by the present and forthcoming challenges. That has led to a series=
 of harebrained strategies and tactics=E2=80=94paying people more to do <i>=
much</i> less, paying them not much more to do <i>far </i>more; the aforeme=
ntioned multiheaded management structures that ensure <i>nothing </i>gets a=
ccomplished; splitting newsrooms; signing sucker deals with A.I. models; wh=
imsically shifting paywall strategies=E2=80=A6 I could go on. In his note t=
o his staff, VandeHei acknowledged that =E2=80=9Cthis is the most difficult=
 moment for media in our lifetime.=E2=80=9D At least someone is willing to =
say the quiet part out loud.<br/></td>
